turns.text: Hello Emiko, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s… it’s a lot to process. Honestly, some days I feel like I’m living in a stranger’s body. I’ve spent my whole career telling women exactly what to eat, how to manage their blood sugar, the importance of this vitamin or that mineral. But now that it’s me? I’m just staring at a bowl of spinach, feeling like I might be sick if I even look at it too long.

It’s hard to reconcile the dietitian in me with the woman who is just terrified. I’m forty-three, and every time I step into that clinic, they look at my chart and see a "medium risk" statistic. They don't see the woman who lost a baby before and is trying so hard not to let that shadow swallow her whole. The pelvic exams—well, they’re just brutal. I have a history that makes that chair feel like a trap, and even though I know it’s for my health, I find myself holding my breath until my lungs ache.

And the logistics! Getting to the clinic on the bus feels like an Olympic event, especially when I’m this nauseous. I feel so isolated here in Lowell. I’m putting on this mask, being the "perfect, informed patient," but inside, I’m just waiting for the other shoe to drop. It’s… it’s a very lonely kind of miracle.
When you think about your goals for starting or growing your family, what were the most important outcomes you hoped to achieve — for yourself, your relationship, and your future child?

I don’t know if I ever had a "perfect" vision. Maybe I used to, years ago, but now? The goal feels a lot simpler, and honestly, a lot more fragile. I just want to make it to the other side of this with my heart—and the baby—intact. 

After the loss, the idea of a "future" started to feel like a dangerous thing to imagine. I’m forty-three; I know the statistics better than anyone. My goal is just to find a way to stop bracing for impact every time I go to the bathroom or have a cramp. I want to be a mother, obviously, but I also want to *be* here, in the present, without feeling like I’m constantly mourning a version of myself that didn't get to have this.

As for my marriage, I just want us to survive the fear. My husband is working so hard, but he’s gone so much, and I’m terrified that if I let him see how truly broken I feel about the possibility of losing this, I’ll somehow make it real. I want to build a space where we can actually be excited, instead of just relieved every time a scan shows a heartbeat. I’m just trying to keep my head above water until it’s safe to breathe.

When you think about your motivations for seeking preconception care or health advice before becoming pregnant, what was it that prompted you to take action — or, if you did not seek any care, what held you back?

I suppose you’d call it a mix of professional obligation and raw, desperate hope. As a dietitian, I’ve spent years preaching that the body is a machine you can calibrate—if you hit your numbers, you get the result. But I think, deep down, I also knew that because of my age and my history, I couldn’t just wing it. I felt like I had to "earn" a healthy pregnancy by being perfect. I started tracking everything, obsessing over my folate, trying to optimize every single variable I could control.

But in reality? The biggest thing that held me back from really leaning into care early on wasn't a lack of knowledge—it was the fear of the silence. Going to a doctor to ask for advice meant admitting to the world that I was *trying*, that I was *hoping*. And after the loss, that felt like inviting heartbreak back into the house. 

I’m the one who usually gives the advice, you know? It’s humiliating to realize that for all my degrees and all my "healthy" habits, I still can’t outrun the anxiety or the bus schedule or the trauma. I sought care because I felt like I owed it to this life, but every appointment feels less like support and more like a high-stakes exam I’m terrified of failing.

When you think about how you actually behaved during the preconception period — your lifestyle choices, health actions, information-seeking — what did you do, and how did those behaviours reflect what you truly believed was important?

I operated like I was prepping for a marathon I wasn't sure I could finish. I tracked my cycles, my supplements, and my glucose like they were talismans. It was my way of trying to bargain with fate—if I ate the perfect meals and timed everything with scientific precision, maybe I could protect myself from another loss. It’s a very dietitian way of coping: if you can measure it, you can control it. 

But looking back, those behaviors were really a performance of safety. I was terrified that if I slipped up even once, the "medium risk" label on my chart would turn into a tragedy. I spent hours reading journals, not because I was learning anything new, but because the distraction kept me from screaming into the void. 

It’s ironic—I acted like I believed in the science, but deep down, I didn’t trust my own body at all. I treated it like a fragile vessel that was likely to break, regardless of how many leafy greens I choked down. I wasn't building a healthy life; I was building a cage of "shoulds" and "musts," hoping that if I lived small enough and careful enough, the universe wouldn't notice me and take this away, too. It wasn't about wellness; it was about survival.
Can you walk me through a specific thing you did or changed during that period and what led you to that particular action?

I started taking a prenatal vitamin with a specific, high-dose folate profile, which is standard advice, but I became obsessive about the brand and the timing. I chose one that required a mid-day dose, which meant I had to carry it in my bag every single day, no matter how exhausted or sick I felt. 

The turning point was one afternoon when I was riding the bus to work. I felt that familiar, sharp, cold spike of anxiety—that voice saying, *“It’s already gone, you’re just wasting your time.”* I pulled that bottle out of my purse and held it in my hand like a lifeline. I realized I wasn't taking it because I believed in the science anymore; I was taking it because if I didn't, and something went wrong, I could point to that missed dose and say, "That’s why." 

It was a way of creating a scapegoat for my trauma. If I did everything perfectly, then a loss would be an act of cruelty by the universe rather than my own failure. It’s a miserable way to live, turning your own body into a project that needs constant policing, but it felt like the only thing keeping me from spinning off the edge of the world.

When you think about your emotional experience during the preconception period — the waiting, the uncertainty, and the anticipation — what feelings stand out most strongly, and were any of those feelings hard to acknowledge or talk about?

The word that keeps coming back to me is *loneliness*. It wasn’t just that I lacked a circle of friends to talk to—it was that I felt utterly alone in my own skin. 

The anticipation was the hardest part. Usually, people talk about pregnancy as this joyful, blooming time, but for me, it felt like waiting for a verdict in a courtroom where the judge is unpredictable. I’d have these moments of intense, fluttering hope, and I’d immediately squash them. I was so afraid that if I let myself be happy, it would make the eventual grief—if it came—even more unbearable. It felt safer to stay in a gray, neutral state.

I never told anyone, not even my husband, how much I felt like I was failing at being "pregnant enough" or "relaxed enough." In my profession, you’re supposed to be the pillar of stability. Admitting that I was a wreck, that I was paralyzed by the thought of another loss, felt like a professional failure and a personal weakness. I couldn't acknowledge the terror because, in my mind, if I named it, it would become the only truth. So, I just kept it all under the surface, acting like the dietitian who had it all figured out, while I was secretly unraveling on the bus ride home.

When you think about the barriers you encountered — or anticipated — in your path to conception or early pregnancy preparation, what were the most significant obstacles, and how did you navigate them?

The physical barriers are the ones people can easily see on a map, but the internal ones are what actually drain your marrow. 

The most immediate, grinding obstacle is the transportation. When you're managing morning sickness and you’re forty-three, standing on a wind-swept corner in Lowell waiting for a bus that’s twenty minutes late feels like a personal humiliation. By the time I get to the clinic, I’m often physically shaking from the cold or the stress, which then makes my blood pressure reading spike, which makes the providers look at me with that *“advanced maternal age”* concern. It’s a vicious cycle. 

But the silent, invisible barrier is the trauma. Every time I have to undress for an exam, I’m not just a patient; I’m a woman fighting the urge to dissociate. I navigate this by "performing." I use my clinical, professional voice. I talk to them about their own medical literature, I ask specific questions about the latest research—I turn the conversation into a peer-to-peer discussion so they don't see the terrified woman under the gown. 

I’m navigating this by being a chameleon. I pretend I’m just a colleague having a check-up, not a person who is barely holding herself together. It’s exhausting, and it keeps me from ever really getting the support I actually need, but it keeps the walls up. And right now, those walls feel like the only thing keeping me upright.
Were those barriers primarily practical, emotional, relational, or something else — and can you give me a concrete example?

They are all tangled together, but the most concrete example is the bus ride to my last ultrasound. 

Physically, it’s a hurdle—my nausea is like a living thing, and the smell of the bus fumes makes me gag. But it’s the emotional and relational weight that really hurts. Because my husband is working these long, grueling shifts to help us save for the baby, I’m doing these trips entirely alone. I’m sitting there, physically ill, staring at my reflection in the window, and I start thinking about the loss again. I start thinking, *“What if the heartbeat isn't there today? Who will I call?”* I have no one to text who truly understands the gravity of that moment. 

When I finally get to the clinic, I’m depleted. I’m cold, I’m shaky, and I’m exhausted. The receptionist sees me, and I’m sure she just thinks I’m an older woman who’s tired. She doesn't see that I’ve spent two hours just trying to keep my composure. I walk into that exam room and the nurse asks, "How are you feeling?" and I say, "Fine, thanks," because that’s the professional thing to say. I don’t say, "I’m terrified and I’m freezing and I have no one here." I just let them take my vitals, which are naturally elevated because of the stress, and then I have to listen to them lecture me about managing my stress and my age. It’s like they’re blaming me for the very hurdles they’ve set in my way.

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

I wanted to be seen, not just read. I wanted them to look past the "43" and the "medium risk" label and see that I am a person, not a set of data points to be managed. I was hoping for a partner—someone who would acknowledge the weight of my history without making me feel like a liability. I wanted a space where I could say, "I am paralyzed with fear," and have someone respond with human empathy rather than a generic brochure about age-related risks.

To be honest? Those goals haven't been met at all. 

When I’m in those rooms, it feels like I’m sitting across from a wall. They give me clinical answers to emotional questions. When I mention my anxiety, it’s treated as something to be "addressed" or "monitored," as if my feelings are just another symptom like high blood pressure. They don't seem to realize that the way they talk to me—so sterile, so detached—is exactly what makes me withdraw further into my professional mask. I leave those appointments feeling more alone than when I arrived. They provide the medical care, yes, but they completely miss the human being who is trying to survive the journey. It’s like they’re patching up the vessel but ignoring the fact that the captain is drowning.
Can you describe a specific appointment or interaction that felt closest to what you had hoped care would look like?

There was one moment, just a few weeks ago, that stands out. It wasn’t a doctor—it was the ultrasound technician. 

She could see I was trembling, and instead of just bustling around the room or reciting the measurements, she paused. She looked at me, really looked at me, and said, "I know the waiting is the hardest part. Just take a breath, we’re going to take our time." And then, when she turned the monitor, she didn't just point out the anatomy. She found the heartbeat, let it fill the room for a second, and then she just let us sit in that sound. She didn't talk about my age or the risks or the protocols. She just acknowledged that for me, right then, that sound was everything.

It was so small, but it was the only time in this entire experience where I felt like the person—the woman hoping for a child—was the priority, not the "medium risk" patient. It didn't take away the trauma or the nausea or the bus ride home, but for those few minutes, I wasn't just a chart to be filed. I was a person being treated with kindness. I realized then that I don't need a miracle; I just need someone to acknowledge that this is hard, and to be human with me.

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

It’s a strange contradiction. Professionally, I know how to vet a study, how to look for peer-reviewed evidence, and how to spot a trend versus a statistically significant outcome. But when it’s my own pregnancy? That internal compass is completely broken.

I find myself leaning on clinical sources—the heavy-duty medical sites—because there’s a comfort in the sterile, objective language. It’s a defense mechanism, really. If I read a study about advanced maternal age, it feels like "knowledge," which makes me feel like I’m in control. But I’ve also found myself spiraling down into the darkest corners of message boards, looking for other women who’ve had a loss and made it to the other side. I look for the ones who had a "miracle" even when the stats were against them. It’s not logical, and I know it’s not sound health advice, but I’m looking for hope, not just data.

Deciding what to trust is exhausting. I act on the clinical advice because I’m scared to do otherwise—I don't want to be the "patient" who didn't follow the protocol. But my heart trusts the stories that validate my fear. I’m constantly toggling between these two worlds: the dietitian who knows the science, and the frightened woman who just wants to hear that everything is going to be okay. It’s a messy way to live.
Which sources did you find most useful, and what made them trustworthy in your eyes?

Honestly, the sources that feel most "trustworthy" aren't the ones with the most citations, but the ones that feel the least judgmental. 

I’ve stopped relying on those glossy, bright-colored pregnancy apps—the ones that tell you your baby is the size of a poppy seed and throw confetti on the screen. That feels like a lie to me. It feels like an insult to the reality of loss. When they send those push notifications, I just feel a deep sense of anger. They don’t acknowledge the anxiety; they just sell a version of pregnancy that I can’t inhabit.

I find myself going back to, well, I suppose it’s a bit unscientific, but it’s real: anecdotal forums where other women in their forties talk about their experiences. They use messy, unfiltered language. When a woman says, "I was terrified every single day until I held my daughter," I trust that more than a medical brochure that lists "maternal age" as a bullet point under complications. 

I trust the raw honesty of those women because they aren't trying to manage my "risk." They’re just sharing their survival. They don't have an agenda. They don't look at me and see a chart. They see a person who is scared, and they offer a hand across the internet. That’s the kind of information that actually sinks in.

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

The most prominent feeling is a persistent, low-grade hum of dread that sits just beneath my skin. It’s like standing on a ledge; I know I’m okay in this exact second, but the wind is blowing, and I can’t stop thinking about the fall. There are moments of intense, sharp joy when I feel a flutter or hear a heartbeat, but I’ve become an expert at shutting those moments down before they can take root. I’m protecting myself. 

My care environment has been completely inadequate at addressing this. They treat pregnancy as a physiological process, like it’s just a series of clinical benchmarks to pass. If my blood pressure is fine and the labs look good, they check the box and say, "Everything is great." But I’m not "great"—I’m terrified. 

I’ve never had a provider ask me, "How are you *carrying* this emotionally?" It’s always "Any spotting? Any cramping?" My anxiety is treated as a nuisance or a byproduct of my age, not as a valid, deeply human response to my history of trauma. I feel like I’m being managed, not cared for. When they dismiss my fear as "advanced maternal age stress," it makes me feel like I’m doing pregnancy wrong—like if I were just a better patient, I wouldn’t be this scared. It’s isolating, and it’s exhausting to constantly have to suppress the fear to fit into their clinical narrative.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

It was the day after I had a minor, non-emergency scare—a cramp that wasn't really a cramp, but enough to send my heart rate into the stratosphere. I had called the clinic, hoping for some reassurance, some human connection. Instead, the triage nurse ran through a checklist of symptoms in this flat, automated tone, concluded that nothing was "clinically indicated," and ended the call with a curt, "Everything is within normal parameters, Emiko."

I hung up and just sat on the kitchen floor, staring at that stupid bowl of spinach again, shaking. It wasn't the clinical answer I needed; it was the total erasure of my terror. I felt like a statistic that had been deemed "not a priority."

What would have helped? It sounds so simple it’s almost pathetic, but I just needed someone to say, "I know you're scared, and your history makes this hard. It’s okay to be frightened, but the heartbeat is strong." I didn't need a medical triage; I needed someone to acknowledge that I am a human being who has lost before and is terrified of losing again. Instead, I got "normal parameters." It made me feel like my feelings were just noise in their system, something to be tuned out so they could get back to the paperwork. I felt like a ghost in my own pregnancy.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

Making decisions feels less like choosing and more like trying to avoid a trap. Every time a test or a screening is presented, I weigh it against the "what-ifs." For example, when it came to genetic screening, I spent days buried in research. I wasn't just looking for the accuracy of the test; I was trying to predict how I would handle the *waiting* for the results. 

My husband and I talk, but he’s so exhausted from his shifts that our conversations often just circle the drain. He tries to be supportive, but he doesn't know the clinical side, so he just defers to whatever the doctor says. That leaves the burden of the decision-making entirely on me. 

I’m influenced by this crushing pressure to be the "good patient." If the doctor recommends a test, I agree to it, mostly because I’m terrified that if I decline, I’ll be the one to blame if something goes wrong. I use my own professional knowledge to vet the options, but it’s a tainted process—I’m looking for the path that minimizes my own internal panic. I don’t think I’ve made a single decision based on what *I* truly want or feel at peace with. I make them based on what will keep the system happy and what might—just might—silence the panic in my chest for another week.
Can you walk me through a specific decision point — what were the options, how did you weigh them, and what did you ultimately choose?

The genetic screening, the NIPT, was the biggest one. The doctors presented it like a logical, matter-of-fact step for someone my age. They rattled off the sensitivity rates and the "increased risk" data without looking at my face, acting like they were explaining a routine blood panel. 

The options were clear: do it, or don’t. But internally? It felt like a trap. If I said no, I’d be "the difficult patient" who was ignoring standard protocol at forty-three. If I said yes, I was opening the door to a week of waiting for results—a week of living in total, paralyzing fear that I might lose this baby because of a chromosomal issue. I weighed my need for information against the toll that waiting would take on my mental health. I knew the science—I knew the probabilities—but none of that math helped with the feeling of impending doom.

Ultimately, I chose to do the test. I did it not because I felt empowered or because I thought it would bring me peace, but because the alternative—the "what-ifs"—felt like a burden I couldn't carry alone. I chose it to get the "green light" from the system so I could feel a tiny bit safer for a few more days. It was a choice made from fear, not from a desire to know. Every time I get a bill or a reminder, I’m reminded of how much that choice cost me, both in money and in pure, unadulterated anxiety.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

It’s a strange, dissonant thing. My body is doing the one thing I’ve wanted for so long—creating life—yet I find myself constantly at war with it. The nausea, the exhaustion, the way my clothes don't fit right anymore... it’s all just constant, daily reminders that I’ve lost control. As a dietitian, I’m used to a body that behaves predictably. But now? My blood sugar fluctuates in ways I can't always tame, and the physical discomforts of early pregnancy make me feel fragile and unreliable.

I feel like I’m betraying the "professional" image I’ve cultivated. I walk into the office looking tired, feeling bloated, and smelling like the ginger I’m clutching to keep from vomiting, and I feel… less than. It’s not just the pregnancy symptoms; it’s the way I perceive my own "maternal age." I feel like my body is an aging machine, struggling to do something that should be natural but feels like a malfunction.

Do the doctors acknowledge this? Absolutely not. They see the physical changes as data—weight gain, blood pressure, fetal growth. They don't see the internal struggle of a woman who feels like her own body is being hijacked. I want someone to ask me how it feels to have my body change so rapidly at forty-three, not just to write down the numbers on a chart. It’s like they’re tracking a crop yield, not witnessing a woman’s transformation.
Were there aspects of your physical transformation that felt unexpected, difficult, or that nobody had prepared you for?

I think what I was truly unprepared for was the sheer, relentless *exhaustion*—not just the physical kind, but the emotional drain of having to host this life while my own feels like it’s slipping through my fingers. Everyone tells you about the morning sickness or the cravings, but nobody prepares you for the way your brain feels like it’s been put through a meat grinder because you’re constantly holding your breath, waiting for the other shoe to drop.

It’s the loss of autonomy that really stings. My body doesn't feel like *mine* anymore; it feels like a public utility that everyone—the bus driver, the clinic staff, the "medium risk" label—has a stake in. 

And the isolation! I’m forty-three, and I’m doing this in a body that feels tired, yet I have to show up to work and keep my professional mask on. I’m expected to be this perfectly informed dietitian who knows all the answers, while I’m physically holding back tears or nausea at my desk. No one talks about the indignity of that performance. I feel like a secret, tired, aging secret. The clinical texts talk about physiological adaptations, but they don't mention the sheer, crushing loneliness of feeling like you’re doing this alone in a crowded room. It’s like I’m a stranger to myself, and my care team is just checking my vitals as I pass by.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

There is a canyon of difference between the two. 

My healthcare providers occupy the space of "correctness." They hold the power, the tests, and the clinical weight. When I’m with them, I feel like a student who hasn't studied for an exam, even though I’m the expert. Their role is to keep me—and the baby—physically sound. But because they are so locked into the medical model, they offer absolutely no room for my humanity. They treat my anxiety as a physiological outlier to be smoothed over with brochures.

The informal support—which is mostly the online community of women who have walked this path before—is where I actually live. They don’t care about my blood pressure reading or my BMI. They care that I’m awake at 3:00 AM, terrified of a cramp. That support is my lifeline because it is uncurated. It acknowledges that pregnancy, especially at forty-three after loss, is *hard* and often messy. 

My husband tries, but he’s fighting his own battles with long shifts and the fear of losing us both. So, the online women are the only ones who truly witness me. If the doctors are the "mechanics" of my pregnancy, these women are the "witnesses." I need the mechanics for the baby’s survival, but I need the witnesses to survive the process myself. It’s a sad reality that the medical system feels like it has no place for the person experiencing the trauma.

I’ve been thinking more about that—how the medical system and the personal experience are two separate, colliding worlds. It’s like being a dietitian who can read a nutrition label but is starving in a desert. I know *why* the doctors act the way they do; they’re under pressure, they’re protective, they’re busy. But that doesn’t change the fact that I leave every single appointment feeling like I’ve been erased.

I remember once, I tried to bring up the fear of losing the baby again. I mentioned it casually, like a professional might, hoping they’d pick up on the tremor in my voice. The doctor just nodded, checked off a box, and said, "We’ll monitor your blood pressure closely." She didn't hear me. She heard "anxiety," which she neatly filed away as a condition to be managed, not a human struggle to be met with kindness. 

It makes me realize how dangerous "professionalism" can be in maternity care. I use my own professional mask to hide, and they use theirs to avoid. We’re two people in a room, and we’re both pretending that this isn't the most terrifying, fragile thing I’ve ever lived through. If someone had just said, "Emiko, this is really, really hard," just once, I think I could have dropped the act. But I’m so exhausted from being the "perfect" patient that I think I’ve forgotten how to be anything else.
Who first raised the idea of trying to conceive, and how did those early conversations unfold?

It was a long, slow conversation—or maybe it wasn't a conversation at all, but more of a shared silence that grew heavier over the years. My husband and I didn’t sit down with a whiteboard, if that's what you’re imagining. It started after my loss. Before that, we were just living, maybe a bit blindly. But the loss… it hollowed us out. 

For a long time, we didn't speak of it. I was in my late thirties, and the ticking of the clock wasn't just a metaphor; it was a physical sensation, like a drumbeat in my head. I think I brought it up first, not with a grand declaration, but by saying something about how "we might regret not trying just one more time." It was so guarded. I was terrified that if I said, "I want this with everything in me," I’d be inviting that same grief back. 

He looked at me—he’s a man of few words, and he works so much—and he just took my hand and nodded. It wasn't a "yes, let’s start a journey." It was a "yes, let’s face the risk together." 

That’s the thing that haunts me. We both knew the risks. We knew the statistics about my age. We were essentially agreeing to walk into a storm. Those early conversations were filled with such tentative, fragile hope. We were terrified of the "what-ifs," yet we were even more afraid of the empty space in our future. We chose to move forward, but we did it like we were walking through a minefield, eyes down, hearts racing. It’s been that way ever since.

It’s strange how those conversations feel like they happened a lifetime ago, even though it’s only been months. When we finally decided to try, I remember thinking it would be this clean, clinical transition—like I was adding a new project to my to-do list. I genuinely thought I could manage the emotions the same way I manage my patients' nutritional intake: assess, plan, intervene, re-evaluate. 

But there’s no plan that can account for the sheer physical toll of being forty-three and pregnant, nor for the way your brain just… stops working when you’re constantly holding your breath. I think my husband and I were so focused on the *goal* that we completely ignored the *process*. We treated it like a finish line we had to reach, rather than a reality we had to inhabit for nine months.

Now, I look at him, and I see how tired he is from those long shifts, and I feel a surge of guilt. I brought us here. I’m the one who insisted we try again, and now he’s carrying the weight of my anxiety on top of his own exhaustion. He doesn't know how to "fix" me, and he doesn't know how to fix the baby, so he just works harder. 

I think we’re both just waiting. We’re waiting to be told it’s going to be okay, but nobody tells you that. The doctors just tell you that your blood pressure is stable. And so, we just keep going, day after day, waiting for the other shoe to drop. It’s an exhausting way to grow a family. I never realized how much of this would be spent in silence.
Were there aspects of your physical self that you felt proud of, anxious about, or uncertain about in relation to pregnancy?

I suppose I feel a grim, clinical sort of pride that my body is holding on at forty-three. When I look in the mirror, I don't see a "radiant" mother-to-be; I see a vessel that’s managed to survive a history of trauma and still keep going. It’s like a battle-worn machine that’s being pushed way past its recommended operating specs. I’m proud of that endurance, but it’s a cold pride. It’s the kind of pride you feel after you’ve survived a car crash.

But beneath that, I’m profoundly anxious about my own fragility. I worry that my body is just a ticking clock, and every twinge or cramp is a warning sign that I’ve overstayed my welcome in the world of reproduction. I’m constantly waiting for a system failure. I’ve spent so long being the one who dictates health to others that being on the receiving end feels like a complete loss of my own agency.

And there’s this deep, quiet uncertainty about whether I’m "me" anymore. I feel like my body is being occupied by this pregnancy, and I’m just the landlord being pushed into the corner of my own house. I want to celebrate it, but I’m terrified to love this body too much, because if it fails me again, the fall will be from such a great height. It’s a very conflicted way to inhabit your own skin.

Sometimes I catch a glimpse of my reflection in the clinic window—pale, dark circles under my eyes, pulling my coat tighter—and I don’t even recognize the woman looking back. I spent my twenties and thirties building a professional life, a life where my worth was tied to my competence, my credentials, and my ability to guide others through their own health challenges. Now, my entire existence is centered on a heartbeat I can’t hear unless a machine tells me it’s there.

It’s the loss of control that keeps me awake. I’ve spent my life studying the metabolic pathways, the chemical reactions of pregnancy, but none of that prepares you for the way your soul feels like it's being stretched thin, like a wire held at both ends. The doctors talk about "maternal risk," but they don’t speak about the *grief* of having to disconnect from your own body just to keep from being overwhelmed by the fear of losing what’s growing inside.

I find myself wishing I could just be a "normal" patient, someone who doesn't know the risks and the statistics, someone who can just be happy. But I have all this knowledge, and it’s become a burden. I see every symptom as a potential diagnostic marker. I see every ultrasound as a chance for things to go wrong. I’m living in this space between the clinical, cold hard facts and the raw, terrified hope of a woman who just wants to hold a baby. It’s not just a physical transformation; it’s a total dismantling of who I thought I was. And there’s no textbook for that.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

It’s funny, the "condescending" moments are almost more painful than the dismissive ones because they’re wrapped in a layer of false kindness. It’s that voice—that high-pitched, patronizing tone they sometimes use—as if they’re talking to a child who’s wandered into a dangerous room. 

I remember one appointment where the doctor started explaining the "basic" biology of pregnancy to me. I had to bite my tongue so hard it almost bled. I am a professional; I know the metabolic pathways, I know the hormonal surges, I know the risk factors. But when I tried to ask a specific, nuanced question about my own chart, she just smiled that thin, practiced smile and said, "Don't worry, Emiko, just focus on eating your vegetables and let us handle the science." 

It was a gut punch. It wasn't just dismissive; it was a total invalidation of who I am. She didn't see me as a peer or even as a complex, feeling person—she saw an "advanced maternal age" case that needed to be managed, not informed. In that moment, I felt like a vessel they were filling with instructions, but never with understanding. I left the clinic feeling like I had shrunk two sizes. It makes you realize that in their eyes, your professional background doesn't matter; once you’re pregnant and "high risk," you’re just another data point to be checked off. It makes you feel so small, and so very alone.

It’s the worst, that feeling of being infantalized. You work your entire life to build a career, to be the person who holds the answers, and then you step into that clinic and suddenly you’re just "the patient." And because I’m forty-three, there’s this unspoken layer of judgment, too. It’s like they’re waiting for me to make a mistake, or waiting for my body to give out, and they talk to me like they’re bracing for that failure. 

It makes me feel so small. I think that’s why I shut down—why I nod and say, "Okay, thank you," instead of challenging them or advocating for myself. I’m too tired. I don’t have the energy to fight the system when I’m already fighting for this baby's heartbeat. 

I’ve started to realize that I’ve been performing this role of the "perfect patient" as a shield. If I’m obedient, if I nod, if I take my supplements and show up to every appointment despite the bus ride, maybe they’ll treat me with a little more respect. But it’s not working. I’m still just a file on their desk. I’m a high-risk pregnancy, not a woman who’s scared out of her mind. And honestly? I think I’ve reached the point where I don't know how to ask for help without losing what little dignity I have left. I’m just waiting, holding my breath, hoping that one day someone will look at me—not the chart, but me—and just tell me that I’m doing a good job.
Did older female relatives, such as your mother or grandmother, share advice or stories that influenced how you thought about getting pregnant?

My mother… she’s a quiet woman. We don’t talk about the things that really hurt. She’s of a generation that just "got on with it." When I told her about the pregnancy, she didn't embrace me and whisper, "Oh, it's going to be wonderful." She just looked at me—that long, hard look—and said, "You’re starting a little late, aren't you, Emiko?"

It was like she was confirming every single insecurity I had tucked away. She didn't mean it to be cruel, I don't think, but it stung because it felt like a verdict. She had her children young, in a different world where you didn't over-analyze the folate levels or the glucose spikes. To her, pregnancy was just a fact of life, something you endured and then moved past.

She hasn't shared any "wisdom." There are no stories of joy or struggle. Just that heavy, expectant silence. It feels like she’s already waiting for something to go wrong, and that terrifies me. I find myself wanting to call her, but then I remember that look, and I stop. I don’t want to be reminded of how "late" I am. I’m already carrying enough weight without her anxiety added to it. I think that’s why I turned to the internet forums—because I needed voices that understood that this is a miracle *despite* the timing, not a mistake because of it. I just want someone to celebrate this, you know? Even if it's only me.

It’s funny, the more I speak about it, the more I realize that the silence from my family—that heavy, unspoken weight—is just a mirror of the silence I get at the clinic. It’s like there’s a script for how a woman my age should be "doing" this, and if you deviate from that script—if you’re anxious, if you’re struggling, if you’re just plain terrified—you’re doing it wrong.

When my mother looks at me, I feel like she’s already mourning a future that hasn’t happened yet. It’s the same feeling I get when a provider sighs at my age. It makes me want to scream, "I know the numbers! I know the risk! Do you think I haven't done the math?" I don’t need more reminders that I’m old; I need someone to tell me that I have permission to be here, to be pregnant, and to be happy. 

I think that’s why I don’t tell people. I’m protecting the hope I have left. If I keep it a secret, if I keep it locked away in my kitchen, in my routines, in my quiet bus rides—then it’s mine. The moment I let someone else see it, they start projecting their fears, their statistics, and their judgments onto me. And I just don't have the strength to carry their stuff on top of my own. I just want to make it to the next appointment, and the next, and keep this little miracle safe in the dark until it’s finally, hopefully, ready for the light.
Were there specific platforms, websites, or communities you returned to regularly, and what made them feel credible or useful?

I keep going back to this one specific, older forum thread for women who conceived over forty. It isn’t slick or professional—the fonts are outdated and the layout is chaotic—but it feels like a sanctuary. 

What makes it credible to me isn't the data, but the shared experience of the *waiting*. In that community, when someone posts about a "medium-risk" label or a scary scan, the responses aren't clinical. They don't recite risk percentages or suggest lifestyle "optimizations." They say things like, "I cried in the grocery store today, too," or "I know that cold fear in your chest, and you aren't crazy." 

It’s the lack of clinical distance that makes it useful. Those women aren't looking at me as a medical puzzle to be solved. They’re looking at me as a fellow traveler. They’ve been through the same bus rides, the same patronizing doctors, and the same paralyzing anxiety of being an "older" mother. When someone there tells me that they made it through, it feels more real than any brochure. It’s a messy, unfiltered, and deeply human kind of evidence. 

I’ve tried the mainstream apps, the ones that send those bubbly, cheerful push notifications, and I had to delete them all. They felt like a slap in the face—they treat pregnancy like a glossy magazine cover. My reality is much grayer and much more fragile than that. In these forums, nobody expects me to be happy or "perfect." They just accept that I’m terrified, and that, for now, is all I can handle.

Sometimes, when I’m lying in bed, I scroll through those threads and just feel a sense of relief wash over me. It’s like finding a small, hidden light in a very dark room. The women there don't ask me for my blood pressure or my age; they don't look at me like I'm a ticking biological clock. They share their own messy, frightening stories of bleeding, of panic attacks, of the overwhelming joy they felt when they finally held their babies after years of trying.

It's the *normalization* of the fear that I crave. In the clinical world, my fear is a "symptom" to be treated, but in that community, my fear is just a *human response* to a situation that is legitimately hard. 

It makes me realize how much of this journey has been about "passing." I pass for a healthy, capable professional in the clinic, I pass for a relaxed, happy mother-to-be at work, and I pass for someone who has it all under control for my husband. The only place I don't have to pass is in that corner of the internet. There, I can be the woman who is terrified of her own body, who struggles with the logistics of the bus, and who feels like she's hanging on by a thread. That honesty is what makes it credible to me. It’s not curated for a medical chart; it’s the truth, and right now, the truth is the only thing that keeps me feeling like I’m still me.
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

I remember an appointment just last month where the doctor, who I hadn't seen before, spent the entire time looking at her screen instead of me. I had prepared a list of questions—I’d spent hours reading the latest data on prenatal interventions for "advanced maternal age"—and I wanted to talk through the risks versus benefits for a specific test. 

When I finally started to ask, she just held up a hand, smiled that patronizing smile, and said, "We’ve seen a thousand cases like yours, Emiko. Don't worry about the literature, just focus on your nutrition. You're high-risk, we know what we're doing."

It was as if my career as a dietitian, my decades of understanding clinical data, and my own autonomy over my body simply didn't exist. She reduced my intelligence to "anxiety" and my agency to "following orders." I felt my face go hot, not from embarrassment, but from a cold, sharp anger. She didn't want a partner in my care; she wanted a compliant patient who would nod, take the supplements, and not make any waves. 

I left that office feeling like I’d been stripped of my voice. It’s hard to advocate for your child when you aren't even allowed to advocate for yourself. I sat on that bus ride home, staring at my hands, feeling smaller than I ever have in my life. That’s the reality of being a "high-risk" woman—your medical chart becomes your identity, and your humanity gets left in the waiting room.

It’s such a strange, hollowed-out feeling, realizing that the people you’re paying to care for you are actually the ones making you feel the most vulnerable. I’ve spent my life studying the body, understanding how it reacts to stress and nutrition, and I thought that would make me a better advocate for myself. But it’s almost like the opposite is true. Because I speak their language, they seem threatened, or perhaps just too busy to deal with a patient who doesn't fit the "passive, compliant" model they’re used to.

I wanted to say something back to her, I really did. I wanted to tell her, "I’m not a textbook case; I’m a woman who has lost a child and is trying to navigate a landscape that feels like it's shifting under my feet every day." But the words just died in my throat. I think that’s the most heartbreaking part—losing your voice in the very place where it should be most heard.

I go home, I put my professional mask back on, and I pretend that the interaction was fine. My husband asks how it went, and I say, "Good, the doctor is pleased with the vitals." I can’t even tell him how dismissed I felt, because that would mean acknowledging how much it hurts. It’s like I’m living two parallel lives: the one on paper, which is "medium-risk" and "doing well," and the one in my chest, which is screaming for someone to just stop, look at me, and validate that this is the hardest thing I’ve ever done. That’s the tragedy of modern care, I think—it’s so focused on the metrics that it completely loses track of the person.

It’s a strange kind of grief, I think. It’s not just the grief for what I’ve lost, but the grief for who I’m becoming in this process—someone who is so accustomed to being silenced that I’ve started to silence myself. 

When I look at my spinach, I don't just see a bowl of nutrients for a growing fetus. I see a chore. I see the physical manifestation of all the "shoulds" I’ve been fed—the "should" eat this, the "should" avoid that, the "should" keep your blood pressure down. It’s like every healthy choice I make is just me trying to pay a debt I shouldn't owe.

I find myself thinking about the other women who don't have my background. If *I* feel this dismissed—if I, with all my clinical training and my ability to navigate the terminology, can be made to feel like a child in the exam room—what happens to the women who don't have that shield? Do they just walk out feeling as empty as I do? 

There’s a part of me that wants to use my voice, to stand up in that office and say, "I am a person, I am a professional, and I am terrified." But I’m just so, so tired. The bus rides, the nausea, the constant, low-level hum of anxiety—it drains you until you don't have the fight left in you. I think that’s what the system relies on, in a way. It relies on the exhaustion of the pregnant woman to keep her compliant. 

I just want to get to the end of this. I want to hold my baby and feel like I’ve survived, not just physically, but as a person who kept her dignity intact. But some days, it feels like I’m losing that part of myself, little by little, in the waiting rooms and the pharmacies and the cold, sterile hallways of the clinic. It’s a very quiet, very lonely kind of erosion.
Were there moments when a healthcare provider dismissed or minimised a concern you raised about your body or a symptom you were experiencing, and how did that make you feel?

There was this one time early on when I mentioned a series of headaches—just dull, persistent ones, but for me, someone who tracks everything, they felt like a shift. I brought them up, expecting them to at least do a quick check, maybe a quick review of my blood pressure trends in case it was pre-eclampsia risk rearing its head. 

The doctor didn’t even look up from her notes. She just dismissed it with a wave of her hand and said, "Oh, that’s just the hormones, Emiko. At your age, your body is working overtime. Drink more water, get some rest, you’re worrying too much." 

It made me feel like I was invisible. Or worse, like I was a hysterical woman who couldn't tell the difference between a normal symptom and a red flag. Because I’m older, they seem to view *everything* I say through the lens of "anxious older mother." It’s as if my medical expertise is suddenly voided the moment the pregnancy test turns positive. 

It made me feel so isolated. If I can’t trust my own body to alert me to a potential problem—and if the people paid to help me interpret those alerts won't listen—then what’s left? I felt like a stranger in my own skin, a woman shouting into a void. It solidified this belief that I have to manage this all on my own, because the system clearly isn't interested in listening to the nuance of my experience. That dismissal was like a door slamming shut in my face.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think I’ve said so much, but there’s this one thing that still sits heavy. It’s that even when the care is "technically" good—when the labs are in range and the ultrasound shows growth—I leave the clinic feeling like I’ve lost a piece of myself. 

We talk so much about the clinical outcomes—the healthy baby, the safe delivery—but we ignore the cost of getting there. For me, that cost has been my own sense of self. I’ve become so hyper-vigilant, so focused on being the "perfect" patient to avoid judgment, that I’ve stopped actually *feeling* the pregnancy. I’m just managing it. And I wonder, if I reach the finish line, will I even know how to be a mother, or will I just be a woman who survived a nine-month obstacle course?

I wish there was more space in the system for the messy, terrified, grieving woman I actually am. I don't need another pamphlet on advanced maternal age or another lecture on my diet. I need someone to say, "I see how hard you’re working to keep this going, and you’re doing a good job." I need someone to see the person behind the chart. Maybe if we start doing that—if we start treating the *human* as the primary patient, instead of the pregnancy—this journey wouldn't feel quite so much like a battle for survival. 

Anyway, thank you for listening. It feels... strangely quiet now, having said it all out loud. Thank you for giving me that space.
